Slide 13:
Undecidability
Let’s recap and make some notes
To prove that a language A is undecidable:
Assume that A is decidable
Since A is decidable, there exists a TM R that decides A
Next, we try to decide ATM using R as a helper machine
To decide ATM, we need to know whether ⟨M, w⟩∈ATM
In other words, we need to know whether M accepts w
Since R is a decider for the language A, R can tell you
whether a string s is in A
So, we need to construct a string s such that whether s is in
A depending on whether M accepts w
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu
Reducibility 04

Slide 14:
Undecidability
To construct a string s such that whether s is in A depending
on whether M accepts w, there are two choices:
1
Choice #1:
If M accepts w, s ∈A
If M does not accept w, s ̸∈A
2
Choice #2:
If M accepts w, s ̸∈A
If M does not accept w, s ∈A
Which one?
It depends on the problem
For some problems, either choices will work
For some problems, only one of those choices will work
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu

ETM

and a string w) that satisﬁes:

TM M′:

1
Run M on input w
2
If M accepts w, accept. If M rejects w, reject.”
From the above TM M′:
If M accepts w, M′accepts all strings (L(M′) ̸= ∅)
If M does not accepts w:
If M rejects w, M′rejects all strings (L(M′) = ∅)
If M loops indeﬁnitely on input w, M′loops indeﬁnitely on all

Slide 20:
ETM
Prove that ETM = {⟨M⟩| M is a TM and L(M) = ∅} is undecidable.
Proof: Assume that ETM is decidable. Since ETM is decidable, there exists a TM R
that decides ETM. Let construct a TM S (to decide ATM) as follows:
S = “On input ⟨M, w⟩where M is a TM and w is a string:
1
Construct TM M′as follows:
M′= “On input x:
1
Run M on input w
2
If M accepts w, accept. If M rejects w, reject.”
2
Run R on input ⟨M′⟩
3
If R accepts ⟨M′⟩, reject. If R rejects ⟨M′⟩, accept.”
Next, we need to prove that TM S is a decider for ATM.
Prove that if ⟨M, w⟩∈ATM, S accepts ⟨M, w⟩: Assume that ⟨M, w⟩∈ATM.
Since ⟨M, w⟩∈ATM, M is a TM that accepts w. Since M accepts w, from the
deﬁnition of TM M′, M′accepts all strings. In other words, L(M′) ̸= ∅. Since
L(M′) ̸= ∅, ⟨M′⟩̸∈ETM. Since R is a decider for ETM, by running R on
input ⟨M′⟩, R will reject ⟨M′⟩. Since R rejects ⟨M′⟩, S accepts ⟨M, w⟩.
Prove that if ⟨M, w⟩̸∈ATM, S rejects ⟨M, w⟩: Assume that ⟨M, w⟩̸∈ATM.
Since ⟨M, w⟩̸∈ATM, M is a TM that does not accept w. Since M does not
accept w, from the deﬁnition of TM M′, M′accepts no strings. In other words,
L(M′) = ∅. Since L(M′) = ∅, ⟨M′⟩∈ETM. Since R is a decider for ETM, by
running R on input ⟨M′⟩, R will accept ⟨M′⟩. Since R accepts ⟨M′⟩, S rejects
⟨M, w⟩.
This show that TM S is a decider for ATM. Therefore, ATM is decidable ⇝
contradiction. Therefore, ETM is undecidable.

If x is in the form of 0n1n, accept.
2
If x is not in the form of 0n1n, run M on input w.
3
If M accepts w, accept. If M rejects w, reject.”
From the deﬁnition of the above TM M′:
If M accepts w, L(M′) = Σ∗
If M does not accept w:
If M rejects w, L(M′) = {0n1n| n ≥0}
If M loops indeﬁnitely on w, L(M′) = {0n1n| n ≥0}

Slide 5:
REGULARTM is Undecidable
Choice #2
If M accepts w, L(M′) is not regular
If M does not accept w, L(M′) is regular
Here is an example of a TM M′that satisﬁes choice #2:
M′= “On input x:
1
Run M on input w.
2
If M accepts w and x is in the form of 0n1n, accept;
otherwise, reject.
3
If M rejects w, reject.”
From the deﬁnition of the above TM M′:
If M accepts w, L(M′) = {0n1n| n ≥0}
If M does not accept w:
If M rejects w, L(M′) = ∅
If M loops indeﬁnitely on w, L(M′) = ∅
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu
Reducibility 05

Slide 6:
REGULARTM is undecidable (Choice #1)
Proof: Assume that REGULARTM is decidable. Since REGULARTM is decidable,
there exists a TM R that decides REGULARTM. Let construct a TM S as follows:
S = “On input ⟨M, w⟩where M is a TM and w is a string:
1
Construct TM M′as follows:
M′= “On input x:
1
If x is in the form of 0n1n, accept.
2
If x is not in the form of 0n1n, run M on input w.
3
If M accepts w, accept. If M rejects w, reject.”
2
Run R on input ⟨M′⟩.
3
If R accepts ⟨M′⟩, accept. If R rejects ⟨M′⟩, reject.”
Next, we need to prove that TM S is a decider for ATM.
Prove that if ⟨M, w⟩∈ATM, S accepts ⟨M, w⟩: Assume that ⟨M, w⟩∈ATM.
Since ⟨M, w⟩∈ATM, M is a TM that accepts w. Since M accepts w, from the
deﬁnition of TM M′, L(M′) is Σ∗which is a regular language. Since L(M′) is
regular, ⟨M′⟩∈REGULARTM. Since R is a decider for REGULARTM, by
running R on input ⟨M′⟩, R will accept ⟨M′⟩. Since R accepts ⟨M′⟩, S accepts
⟨M, w⟩.
Prove that if ⟨M, w⟩̸∈ATM, S rejects ⟨M, w⟩: Assume that ⟨M, w⟩̸∈ATM.
Since ⟨M, w⟩̸∈ATM, M is a TM that does not accept w. Since M does not
accept w, from the deﬁnition of TM M′, L(M′) is {0n1n| n ≥0} which is not
a regular language. Since L(M′) is not regular, ⟨M′⟩̸∈REGULARTM. Since
R is a decider for REGULARTM, by running R on input ⟨M′⟩, R will reject
⟨M′⟩. Since R rejects ⟨M′⟩, S rejects ⟨M, w⟩.
This show that TM S is a decider for ATM. Therefore, ATM is decidable ⇝
contradiction. Therefore, REGULARTM is undecidable.
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu

Slide 7:
More Fact for Contradiction
So far, we have been using ATM as the mean of contradiction
Because we know from the fact that ATM is undecidable
But now, we have the following undecidable languages:
ATM = {⟨M, w⟩| M is a TM that accepts w}
HALT TM = {⟨M, w⟩| M is a TM that halts on input w}
ETM = {⟨M⟩| M is a TM and L(M) = ∅}
REGULARTM = {⟨M⟩| M is a TM and L(M) is regular}
We can now use those undecidable languages
For example, to show that a language A is undecidable, we can
show that if A is decidable, ETM is decidable
But we can always use ATM
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu
Reducibility 05

Slide 8:
EQTM is Undecidable
Problem: Is there an algorithm to determine whether
languages of two TMs M1 and M2 are identical?
Formally,
EQTM = {⟨M1, M2⟩| M1 and M2 are TMs and L(M1) = L(M2)}
Unfortunately, EQTM is undecidable
As usual, we are going to assume that EQTM is decidable
In other words, given two TMs M1 and M2, we know whether
⟨M1, M2⟩∈EQTM or not
For this language, we generally need to construct two TMs
M1 and M2 from a TM M and a string w
For this problem, we are going to look at two proofs:
Contradict with the fact that ATM is undecidable
Contradict with the fact that ETM is undecidable
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu

Slide 9:
EQTM is Undecidable
Prove that EQTM = {⟨M1, M2⟩| M1 and M2 are TMs and L(M1) = L(M2)} is
undecidable.
Proof: Assume that EQTM is decidable. Since EQTM is decidable, there exists a TM
R that decides EQTM. Let construct a TM S (to decide ATM) as follows:
S = “On input ⟨M, w⟩where M is a TM and w is a string:
1
Construct TM M1 as follows:
M1 = “On input x:
1
accept.
2
Construct TM M2 as follows:
M2 = “On input x:
1
Run M on input w.
2
If M accepts w, accept. If M rejects w, reject.”
3
Run R on input ⟨M1, M2⟩.
4
If R accepts ⟨M1, M2⟩, accept. If R rejects ⟨M1, M2⟩, reject.”
Next, we need to prove that TM S is a decider for ATM.
Prove that if ⟨M, w⟩∈ATM, S accepts ⟨M, w⟩:
Assume that ⟨M, w⟩∈ATM. Since ⟨M, w⟩∈ATM, M is a TM that accepts w.
Since M accepts w, from the deﬁnition of TM M1, L(M1) = Σ∗and from the
deﬁnition of TM M2, L(M2) = Σ∗. In other words, L(M1) = L(M2). Since
L(M1) = L(M2), ⟨M1, M2⟩∈EQTM. Since R is a decider for EQTM, by
running R on input ⟨M1, M2⟩, R will accept ⟨M1, M2⟩. Since R accepts
⟨M1, M2⟩, S accepts ⟨M, w⟩.
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu
Reducibility 05

Slide 10:
EQTM is Undecidable
Proof: (continue)
Prove that if ⟨M, w⟩̸∈ATM, S rejects ⟨M, w⟩:
Assume that ⟨M, w⟩̸∈ATM. Since ⟨M, w⟩̸∈ATM, M is a TM that does not
accept w. Since M does not accept w, from the deﬁnition of TM M1,
L(M1) = Σ∗and from the deﬁnition of TM M2, L(M2) = ∅. In other words,
L(M1) ̸= L(M2). Since L(M1) ̸= L(M2), ⟨M1, M2⟩̸∈EQTM. Since R is a
decider for EQTM, by running R on input ⟨M1, M2⟩, R will reject ⟨M1, M2⟩.
Since R rejects ⟨M1, M2⟩, S rejects ⟨M, w⟩.
This show that TM S is a decider for ATM. Therefore, ATM is decidable ⇝
contradiction. Therefore, EQTM is undecidable.
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu

Slide 13:
ONE TM is Undecidable
Problem: Given a TM M, is there an algorithm to check
whether M only accept one string (|L(M)| = 1)
Unfortunately, this is another unsolvable problem
Formally,
ONE TM = {⟨M⟩| M is a TM and |L(M)| = 1}
To show that the above problem is unsolvable, we just need to
show that ONE TM is undecidable
Use the same method:
Show that if ONE TM is decidable, ATM is decidable
We need to construct a TM M′from a TM M and a string w

2
Choice #2:
If ⟨M, w⟩∈ATM, ⟨M′⟩̸∈ONE TM
⇝If M accepts w, |L(M′)| ̸= 1
If ⟨M, w⟩̸∈ATM, ⟨M′⟩∈ONE TM

ONE TM is Undecidable
Prove that ONE TM = {⟨M⟩| M is a TM and |L(M)| = 1} is undecidable.
Proof: Assume that ONE TM is decidable. Since ONE TM is decidable, there exists a
TM R that decides ONE TM. Let construct a TM S (to decide ATM) as follows:
S = “On input ⟨M, w⟩where M is a TM and w is a string:
1
Construct TM M′as follows:
M′= “On input x:
1
If x = ε, run M on input w. If M accepts w, accept; otherwise, reject.
2
If x ̸= ε, reject.
2
Run R on input ⟨M′⟩.
3
If R accepts ⟨M′⟩, accept. If R rejects ⟨M′⟩, reject.”
Next, we need to prove that TM S is a decider for ATM.
Prove that if ⟨M, w⟩∈ATM, S accepts ⟨M, w⟩:
Assume that ⟨M, w⟩∈ATM. Since ⟨M, w⟩∈ATM, M is a TM and that
accepts w. Since M accepts w, from the deﬁnition of TM M′, L(M′) = {ε}.
Since L(M′) = {ε}, |L(M′)| = 1. In other words, ⟨M′⟩∈ONE TM. Since R is
a decider for ONE TM, by running R on input ⟨M′⟩, R will accept ⟨M′⟩. Since
R accepts ⟨M′⟩, S accepts ⟨M, w⟩.

ONE TM is Undecidable
Proof: (continue)

Assume that ⟨M, w⟩̸∈ATM. Since ⟨M, w⟩̸∈ATM, M is a TM and that does
not accept w. Since M does not accept w, from the deﬁnition of TM M′,
L(M′) = ∅. Since L(M′) = ∅, |L(M′)| ̸= 1. In other words, ⟨M′⟩̸∈ONE TM.
Since R is a decider for ONE TM, by running R on input ⟨M′⟩, R will reject
⟨M′⟩. Since R rejects ⟨M′⟩, S rejects ⟨M, w⟩.

Slide 17:
Conclusion
So far, to prove that a language A is undecidable, we use
proof by contradiction
Assume that A is decidable
Since A is decidable, there exists a TM R that decides A
Then we show that if A is decidable, ATM is decidable
Another known undecidable language can be used instead of
ATM
So, we need to construct a TM S that decides ATM and prove
that S is a decider for ATM
Given a TM M and a string w, TM S need to construct a
string s such that whether s ∈A depends on whether M
accepts w
Then use TM R (a decider for A) as a helper machine to
decide ATM
Try to prove that ONE TM is undecidable using the choice #2.
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu

Slide 2:
Reductions
Recall the concept of reducibility:
A problem A is reducible to a problem B if the solution to the
problem B can be used to solve the problem A
To show that a language A is undecidable, we show that ATM
(or other undecidable language) is reducible to A
ATM is reducible to HALT TM
We want to know whether ⟨M, w⟩∈ATM
Turn ⟨M, w⟩to ⟨M′, w′⟩(in our case w′= ε)
Check whether ⟨M′, w′⟩∈HALT TM
⟨M, w⟩∈ATM ↔⟨M′, w′⟩∈HALT TM
ATM is reducible to ETM
We want to know whether ⟨M, w⟩∈ATM
Turn ⟨M, w⟩to ⟨M′⟩
Check whether ⟨M′⟩∈ETM
⟨M, w⟩∈ATM ↔⟨M′⟩̸∈ETM
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu

So for we construct TMs to show that ATM is reducible to

Another method is Reduction via Computation Histories
Generally use when the problem to be shown undecidable

Given a set of dominoes, does it has a match?

Slide 5:
Deﬁnitions
Recall deﬁnitions about conﬁguration:
Suppose the start state of TM M is q0, the start
conﬁguration of M on input w is q0w
An accepting conﬁguration is a conﬁguration uqacceptv for
strings u and v
A rejecting conﬁguration is a conﬁguration uqrejectv for
strings u and v
A conﬁguration Ci legally go to Cj according to the
transitions of the TM M
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu
Reducibility 06

Slide 6:
Reductions via Computation Histories
Deﬁnition 5.5
Let M be a Turing machine and w an input string. An accepting
computation history for M on w is a sequence of conﬁgurations,
C1, C2, . . . Cl where C1 is the start conﬁguration of M on w, Cl is
an accepting conﬁguration of M, and each Ci legally follows from
Ci−1 according to the rules of M. A rejecting computation
history for M on w is deﬁned similarly, except that Cl is a
rejecting conﬁguration.
Note that M must halt on input w. Otherwise, accepting or
rejection computation history does not exists
For simplicity, we only consider deterministic Turing machine
If M halts on w, exactly one computation history exists
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu

Slide 7:
Reductions via Computation Histories
Again, to show that a language A is undecidable, we show
that ATM is reducible to A
Given a TM M and a string w, there is no algorithm to check
whether ⟨M, w⟩∈ATM because ATM is undecidable
But we know that
If M accepts w, ⟨M, w⟩∈ATM
If M does not accept w, ⟨M, w⟩̸∈ATM
Using the concept of computation histories:
If the accepting computation history of M on input w exists
⇝M accepts w ⇝⟨M, w⟩∈ATM
If the accepting computation history of M on input w does
not exists ⇝M does not accept w ⇝⟨M, w⟩̸∈ATM
Let’s try to use reduction via computation histories with a
variation of a TM called Linear Bounded Automaton (LBA)
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu

Slide 8:
Linear Bounded Automaton
Deﬁnition 5.6
A linear bounded automaton is a restricted type of Turing
machine wherein the tape head isn’t permitted to move oﬀthe
portion of the tape containing the input. If the machine tries to
move its head oﬀeither end of the input, the head stays where it is.

Slide 9:
Linear Bounded Automaton
Problem: Given an LBA M and a string w, is there an
algorithm to determine whether M accepts w?
Formally,
ALBA = {⟨M, w⟩| M is an LBA that accepts w}
Is ALBA decidable?
This sounds like ATM
ATM = {⟨M, w⟩| M is a TM that accepts w}
Even though ATM is undecidable, ALBA is actually decidable
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu
Reducibility 06

Slide 10:
Linear Bounded Automaton
Recall a conﬁguration uqv
uv is the content of the tape
Recall that very TM has an inﬁnite long tape
Therefore, there are inﬁnite number of distinct strings uv
But the tape of an LBA is ﬁnite
The length of its tape on input string w is |w|
The number of states and |Γ| of an LBA are also ﬁnite
Because of these, the set of all distinct conﬁgurations that an
LBA can have on an input string w is ﬁnite

Slide 11:
Linear Bounded Automaton
Consider an LBA with the following speciﬁcation:
The number of states is q
|Γ| = g (number of symbols in its tape alphabets)
The length of the tape is n
From the above LBA:
For each square on the tape, there can be one of g symbols
There are the total of gnunique tape contents
There are n possible positions of the tape head
For each tape head position, it can be in one of q states
Thus, there are exactly qngndistinct conﬁgurations
Because of this, we can detect when an LBA is entering a
loop by checking whether sequence of conﬁgurations when
running an LBA and a string w contains a duplicate
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu
Reducibility 06

Slide 12:
Linear Bounded Automaton
To detect an inﬁnite loop in an LBA, consider an LBA M
where its series of conﬁguration while processing w is as
follows:
C1, C2, C3, C4, C5, C6, C7, C8, C9, C10, C11, . . .
What if the conﬁguration C3 is the same as C9?
The content of the tape are exactly the same
The current state of M are the same
The tape head are at the same location
Thus
C10 will be the same as C4
C11 will be the same as C5
and so on
However, this method requires us to keep track of sequence of
conﬁgurations
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu

Slide 13:
Linear Bounded Automaton
Recall that if we run an LBA on input w for 1 step, we
already sees two conﬁgurations
The start conﬁguration
The next conﬁguration that legally goes from the start
conﬁguration
Thus, if we run an LBA on input w for k steps, we already see
k + 1 conﬁgurations
Recall that the total number of distinct conﬁgurations is qngn,
we can simply run the LBA for qngnsteps or until it halts
If it does not halt after qngnsteps, we already see qngn+ 1
conﬁgurations
But there are only qngndistinct conﬁgurations
Thus, at least two of them must be the same (loop indeﬁnitely)
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu

Slide 14:
Linear Bounded Automaton
Theorem 5.9
ALBA = {⟨M, w⟩| M is an LBA that accepts string w} is
decidable.
Construct a TM L that decides ALBA as follows:
L = “On input ⟨M, w⟩, where M is an LBA and w is a string:
1
Simulate M on input w for qngnsteps or until it halts.
2
If M has halted, accept if it has accepted and reject if it has
rejected. if it has not halted, reject.”

Slide 15:
ELBA is Undecidable
Now, we just verify that ALBA is decidable
But what about the problem of determining whether an LBA
M accepts no strings (L(M) = ∅)
Formally,
ELBA = {⟨M⟩| M is an LBA and L(M) = ∅}
It turns out that this problem is unsolvable
In other words, ELBA is undecidable
Let’s try to prove it using the same method that we used to
prove that ETM is undecidable to try to prove that ELBA is
undecidable
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu

Slide 16:
ELBA is Undecidable
Assume that ELBA. Since ELBA is decidable, there exists a TM R that decides
ELBA. Construct a TM S (to decide ATM) as follows:
S = “On input ⟨M, w⟩where M is a TM and w is a string:
1
Construct an LBA M′as follows:
M′= “On input x:
1
Run M on input w.
2
If M accepts w, accept; otherwise, reject.”
2
Run R (a decider for ELBA) on input ⟨M′⟩.
3
If R accepts ⟨M′⟩, reject. If R rejects ⟨M′⟩, accept.”
Next, we need to prove that the TM S above, decides ATM. . .
Did you see a problem in the above prove?
M′(constructed by S) must be an LBA
An LBA has a ﬁnite long tape
To run TM M on input w, the LBA M′may need inﬁnite
long tape
You cannot construct an LBA that can always run a TM M
on an input w
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu

Slide 17:
ELBA is Undecidable
In this case, we need to show that ATM is reducible to ELBA
using the computation history method
This method will also give you the same conclusion:
If ELBA is decidable, ATM is decidable
The idea is to construct an LBA B that accepts exactly one
string
The string will be a string representation of the accepting
computation history for M on w
Why this work?
If ⟨M, w⟩∈ATM ⇝M accepts w ⇝the accepting
computation history for M on w exists ⇝LBA B accepts
exactly one string ⇝L(B) ̸= ∅⇝⟨B⟩̸∈ELBA
If ⟨M, w⟩̸∈ATM ⇝M does not accept w ⇝the accepting
computation history for M on w does not exists ⇝LBA B
accepts no strings ⇝L(B) = ∅⇝⟨B⟩∈ELBA
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu
Reducibility 06

Slide 18:
ELBA is Undecidable
But can we construct an LBA that accepts exactly one string
which is the accepting computation history for M on w
without requiring an inﬁnite amount of tape?
Recall that an LBA cannot always runs M on input w because
of its ﬁnite long tape
So, can an LBA checks whether a given string is the accepting
computation history for M on w without actually run M on
input w?
It turns out, we can
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu

Slide 19:
ELBA is Undecidable
An accepting computation history for M on w is a series of
conﬁgurations C1, C2, . . . Cl where
C1 is the start conﬁguration of M on input w
Cl is an accepting conﬁguration
Ci yields Ci+1 according to M
We can represents an accepting computation history by a
single string with the conﬁgurations separated from each
other by the # symbol
#
#
#
#
#
#
C1
C2
C3
Cl
For example an accepting computation history of TM M2 in
Example 3.7 on input 0 can be represented as a single string
as follows:
#q10#⊔q2#⊔⊔qaccept#
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu
Reducibility 06

Slide 20:
ELBA is Undecidable
With an input string x to the LBA B, the machine B must
check the following conditions before accepting x
1
x is in the form of #C1#C2# . . . #Cl# for a number l
2
C1 is the start conﬁguration for M on w
3
Ci+1 legally follows from Ci
4
Cl is an accepting conﬁguration for M
Given the deﬁnition of a TM M and a input string w:
We know the start state of M
Thus, we know the start conﬁguration
We know the transition function of M
Thus, we can check whether Ci+1 legally follows from Ci
Accepting conﬁguration is just uqacceptv for strings u and v
This show that there is an algorithm to check whether an
input string x is an accepting computation history for M on w
without actually running M on input w
Therefore, the LBA B can be constructed
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu

Slide 21:
ELBA is Undecidable
Theorem 5.10
ELBA = {⟨M⟩| M is an LBA and L(M) = ∅} is undecidable.
For the sake of contradiction, assume that ELBA is decidable
There exists a TM R decides ELBA
Construct a TM S decides ATM as follows:
S = “On input ⟨M, w⟩, where M is a TM and w is a string:
1
Construct LBA B from M and w as described in the proof idea
2
Run R on input ⟨B⟩
3
If R rejects ⟨B⟩, accept; if R accepts ⟨B⟩, reject.”
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu
Reducibility 06

Slide 22:
ELBA is Undecidable
Sketch of the proofs:
If ⟨M, w⟩∈ATM:
⇝M accepts w
⇝The accepting computation history for M on w exists
⇝B accepts exactly one string
⇝L(B) ̸= ∅
⇝R rejects ⟨B⟩
⇝S accepts ⟨M, w⟩
If ⟨M, w⟩̸∈ATM:
⇝M does not accept w
⇝The accepting computation history for M on w does not exists
⇝B accepts no strings
⇝L(B) = ∅
⇝R accepts ⟨B⟩
⇝S rejects ⟨M, w⟩
This show that S is a decider for ATM
In other words, ATM is decidable — contradiction
Therefore, ELBA is undecidable
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu

Slide 2:
Reducibility So Far
To prove that ETM is undecidable, we need to show that if
ETM is decidable, then ATM is decidable.
1
Assume that ETM is decidable and obtain its decider R
2
Construct TM S with the help of R to decide ATM
This TM S takes ⟨M, w⟩(an instance of ATM) and construct
a new TM M′
L(M′) ̸= ∅if M accepts w
L(M′) = ∅if M does not accepts w (either rejects or loops
indeﬁnitely)
3
Prove that TM S is a decider for ATM
4
Conclude that ATM is decidable — contradiction
5
Therefore, ETM is undecidable

Slide 3:
Mapping Reducibility So Far
To prove that EQTM is undecidable, we need to show that
ATM is mapping reducible to EQTM
1
Create a computable function f in the form of a TM F
This TM F takes ⟨M, w⟩(an instance of ATM) and construct
two TMs M1 and M2
L(M1) = L(M2) if M accepts w
L(M1) ̸= L(M2) if M does not accepts w (either rejects or
loops indeﬁnitely)
Output ⟨M1, M2⟩(an instance of EQTM)
2
Prove that ⟨M, w⟩∈ATM ↔⟨M1, M2⟩∈EQTM
3
Since ATM is undecidable, EQTM is undecidable
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu

Slide 4:
Reduction via Computation History
To prove that ELBA is undecidable, we need to show that if
ELBA is decidable, then ATM is decidable.
1
Assume that ELAB is decidable and obtain its decider R
2
Construct TM S with the help of R to decide ATM
This TM S take ⟨M, w⟩(an instance of ATM) an construct a
new LBA M′
This LBA M′only accepts the accepting computation history
for M on input w
L(M′) ̸= ∅if M accepts w
L(M′) = ∅if M does not accept w (either rejects or loops
indeﬁnitely)
3
Prove that S is a decider for ATM
4
Conclude that ATM is decidable — contradiction
5
Therefore, ELBA is undecidable

Slide 5:
Common Theme
All three methods so far share a common process
Take ⟨M, w⟩(an instance of ATM)
Construct a new TM, TMs, or LBA
Behaviors of new TM, TMs, or LBA depend on whether M
accepts w
Construct TM or TMs from TM M and string w are quite
straightforward
We can simply run M on input w
Construct LBA from TM M and string w is not quite
straightforward
We cannot simply run M on input w
Use computation history (sequence of conﬁgurations)
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu

Slide 6:
Post Correspondence Problem (PCP)
A Post Correspondence Problem (PCP) is a problem
concerning string manipulation.
Consider a domino
 a
ab

There are two non-empty strings in each domino, one on the
top and one on the bottom.
Given a set of dominoes
( b
ca

,
 a
ab

,
ca
a

,
abc
c
)
Is it possible to make a horizontal line of one or more
dominoes, with duplicates allowed, so that the string obtained
by reading across the top halves matches the one obtained by
reading across the bottom?
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu

To prove that PCP is undecidable, we use the same method
as what we did for other undecidable languages
1

Conclude that ATM is decidable — contradiction

Therefore, PCP is undecidable

Slide 46:
Conclusion
Assume that PCP is decidable. Thus, there exists TM R
decides PCP.
Construct TM S that decides ATM
S =“On input ⟨M, w⟩where M is a TM and w is a string:
1
Construct a set of dominoes P an instance of PCP from TM
M and input w as we discussed
2
Then run R on input ⟨P⟩
3
If R accepts ⟨P⟩, accept
If R rejects ⟨P⟩, reject
To prove that TM S a decider for ATM is straightforward

input:
Slide 47:
Conclusion
Show that TM S is a decider for ATM
1
Assume that ⟨M, w⟩∈ATM. Since ⟨M, w⟩∈ATM, M accepts
w. Since M accepts w, the set of dominoes P constructed in
step 1 will have a match. In other words, ⟨P⟩∈PCP. Since R
is a decider for PCP, by running R on input ⟨P⟩, R will accept
⟨P⟩. Since R accepts ⟨P⟩, S accepts ⟨M, w⟩.
2
Assume that ⟨M, w⟩̸∈ATM. Since ⟨M, w⟩̸∈ATM, M does
not accept w. Since M does not accept w, the set of
dominoes P constructed in step 1 will have not have match. In
other words, ⟨P⟩̸∈PCP. Since R is a decider for PCP, by
running R on input ⟨P⟩, R will reject ⟨P⟩. Since R rejects
⟨P⟩, S rejects ⟨M, w⟩.
This shows that S is a decider for ATM. Therefore, ATM is
decidable — contradiction
PCP is undecidable
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu

Slide 2:
Mapping Reducibility
Deﬁnition 5.20
Language A is mapping reducible to language B, written
A ≤m B, if there is a computable function f : Σ∗→Σ∗, where for
every w,
w ∈A ↔f(w) ∈B.
The function f is called the reduction from A to B.
A
B
f
f
Function f reducing A to B

Slide 3:
Reductions
Crucial Features in a Reduction:
1
For every instance w of the ﬁrst problem that we start with,
we must be able to obtain the instance f(w) of the second
problem algorithmically
In our discussion, this reduction f : Σ∗→Σ∗will be a TM
that produces an output string
2
The answer to the second question for the instance f(w) must
be the same as the answer to the original question for w
w ∈A ↔f(w) ∈B.
In other words, for every w,
If f(w) ∈B, w must be in A
If f(w) ̸∈B, w must not be in A
3
The reduction does not have to be one-to-one or onto as long
as it satisﬁes the above property
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu
Reducibility 08

Slide 4:
Mapping Reducibility
Theorem 5.22
If A ≤m B and B is decidable, then A is decidable.
Proof:
Assume that A ≤m B and B is decidable
Since A ≤m B, there exists a reduction in the form of a TM
F satisfying
w ∈A ↔F(w) ∈B
Since B is decidable, there exists a TM MB that decides B
We just need to use the same method as in Chapter 4 to show
that A is decidable
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu

input:
Slide 5:
Mapping Reducibility
Construct a decider N (to decide A) as follows:
N =“On input w:
1
Run TM F (a reduction from A to B) on w to produce F(w).
2
Run MB (a decider for B) on F(w)
3
If MB accepts F(w), accept. If MB rejects F(w), reject.”
Prove that if w ∈A, N accepts w:
Assume that w ∈A. Since w ∈A, from the property of the reduction F,
F(w) ∈B. Since F(w) ∈B and MB is a decider for B, by running MB on
input F(w), MB will accept F(w). Since MB accepts F(w), N accepts w.
Prove that if w ̸∈A, N rejects w:
Assume that w ̸∈A. Since w ̸∈A, from the property of the reduction F,
F(w) ̸∈B. Since F(w) ̸∈B and MB is a decider for B, by running MB on
input F(w), MB will reject F(w). Since MB rejects F(w), N rejects w.
This shows that N is a decider for A
Therefore A is decidable
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu
Reducibility 08

Slide 6:
Mapping Reducibility
Theorem 5.22
If A ≤m B and B is decidable, then A is decidable.
Let the following:
P ≡A ≤m B
Q ≡B is decidable
R ≡A is decidable
Since (P ∧Q) →R ≡(P ∧¬R) →¬Q, we have
Corollary 5.23
If A ≤m B and A is undecidable, then B is undecidable.
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu

Slide 7:
Mapping Reducibility
Corollary 5.23
If A ≤m B and A is undecidable, then B is undecidable.
We already know from the fact that ATM is undecidable
Because of the above Corollary, to show that a language B is
undecidable, we just need to show that ATM ≤m B
ATM can be replaced by other undecidable language such as
ETM
To show that ATM ≤m B, we need to construct a TM F that
behaves like a reduction from ATM to B satisfying
⟨M, w⟩∈ATM ↔F(⟨M, w⟩) ∈B
Also need to prove that F actually satisﬁes the above property
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu
Reducibility 08

Slide 8:
HALT TM is undecidable
ATM and HALTTM
ATM = {⟨M, w⟩| M is a TM and M accepts w}
HALT TM = {⟨M, w⟩| M is a TM and M halts on input w}
To use mapping reducibility to show that HALT TM is
undecidable:
We need to show that ATM is mapping reducible to HALT TM
(ATM ≤m HALT TM)
Since ATM ≤m HALT TM and ATM is undecidable, from
Corollary 5.23, HALT TM is undecidable
To show that ATM ≤m HALT TM, we need a computable
function F : Σ∗→Σ∗(TM F) such that
⟨M, w⟩∈ATM ↔F(⟨M, w⟩) ∈HALT TM
Note that F(⟨M, w⟩) must be in the form of ⟨M′, w′⟩(the
format of HALT TM)
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu

Slide 9:
HALT TM is undecidable
A computable function F where F(⟨M, w⟩) = ⟨M′, w′⟩is a
TM
A TM that takes ⟨M, w⟩as its input on its tape and produces
the output ⟨M′, w′⟩on its tape after it is halted
Note that TM F must be a reduction from ATM to HALT TM:
⟨M, w⟩∈ATM ↔F(⟨M, w⟩) ∈HALT TM
where F(⟨M, w⟩) = ⟨M′, w′⟩
⟨M, w⟩∈ATM →F(⟨M, w⟩) ∈HALT TM
⇝M accepts w →M′halts on input w′
⟨M, w⟩̸∈ATM →F(⟨M, w⟩) ̸∈HALT TM
⇝M does not accept w →M′does not halt on input w′
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu
Reducibility 08

Slide 10:
HALT TM is Undecidable
Machine F computes a reduction f:
F =“On input ⟨M, w⟩where M is a TM and w is a string:
1
Construct the following machine M′:
M′=“On input x:
1
Run M on w.
2
If M accepts w, accept. If M rejects w, enter inﬁnite loop.”
2
Output ⟨M′, ε⟩.”
Next, we need to check whether TM F satisﬁes
⟨M, w⟩∈ATM ↔⟨M′, w′⟩∈HALT TM
where ⟨M′, ε⟩= F(⟨M, w⟩) (the output of TM F)
To do so, we need to show the following:
1
If ⟨M, w⟩∈ATM, ⟨M′, ε⟩∈HALT TM
2
If ⟨M, w⟩̸∈ATM, ⟨M′, ε⟩̸∈HALT TM

Slide 11:
HALT TM is Undecidable
Show that
⟨M, w⟩∈ATM ↔F(⟨M, w⟩) ∈HALT TM
where F(⟨M, w⟩) = ⟨M′, ε⟩
Assume that ⟨M, w⟩∈ATM. Since ⟨M, w⟩∈ATM, M is a
TM that accepts w. Since M accepts w, from the deﬁnition of
TM M′, M′halts (accepts) on all strings including ε. Since
M′halts on ε, ⟨M′, ε⟩∈HALT TM. Since
⟨M′, ε⟩= F(⟨M, w⟩), F(⟨M, w⟩) ∈HALT TM.
Assume that ⟨M, w⟩̸∈ATM. Since ⟨M, w⟩̸∈ATM, M is a
TM that does not accept w. Since M does not accept w, from
the deﬁnition of TM M′, M′does not halt on all strings
including ε. Since M′does not halt on ε, ⟨M′, ε⟩̸∈HALT TM.
Since ⟨M′, ε⟩= F(⟨M, w⟩), F(⟨M, w⟩) ̸∈HALT TM.
This show that TM F is a reduction from ATM to HALT TM
In other words, ATM ≤m HALT TM
Since ATM is undecidable, HALT TM is undecidable (Corollary
5.23)
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu

Slide 12:
HALT TM is Undecidable
Let’s compare two methods:
Method #1: We construct a TM S to decide ATM as follows:
S = “On input ⟨M, w⟩where M is a TM and w is a string:
1
Construct a TM M′as follows:
M′= “On input x:

ETM is Undecidable
To show that ETM is undecidable using the mapping
reducibility, we need to show that ATM ≤m ETM
In other word, we need to ﬁnd a computable function
F : Σ∗→Σ∗(TM F) such that
⟨M, w⟩∈ATM ↔F(⟨M, w⟩) ∈ETM
Suppose we construct the following TM F:
F = “On input ⟨M, w⟩where M is a TM and w is a string:
1
Construct TM M′as follows:
M′= “On input x:
1
Run M on input w.
2
If M accepts w, accept. If M rejects w, reject.”
2
Output ⟨M′⟩.”
The above TM F does not satisfy the above property
It satisﬁes
⟨M, w⟩∈ATM ↔F(⟨M, w⟩) ∈ETM

ETM is Undecidable
TM F is a reduction from ATM to ETM
This shows that ATM ≤m ETM
By Corollary 5.23, since ATM is undecidable, ETM is
undecidable
Note that if ETM is decidable, ETM is decidable
We discussed this toward the end of Chapter 4
Therefore, ETM is undecidable

Slide 15:
REGULARTM is Undecidable
Use the mapping reducibility to show that
REGULARTM = {⟨M⟩| M is a TM and L(M) is regular} is undecidable.
Solution: We need to construct a reduction F from ATM to REGULARTM satisfying
⟨M, w⟩∈ATM ↔F(⟨M, w⟩) ∈REGULARTM
Construct a TM F as follows:
F = “On input ⟨M, w⟩where M is a TM and w is a string:
1
Construct a TM M′as follows:
M′= “On input x:
1
If x is in the form of 0n1n, accept.
2
If x is not in the form of 0n1n, run M on input w. If M accepts w,
accept; otherwise, reject.”
2
Output ⟨M′⟩.”
Next we need to show that the above TM F satisﬁes the property:
⟨M, w⟩∈ATM ↔⟨M′⟩∈REGULARTM
where ⟨M′⟩= F(⟨M, w⟩) (output of TM F):
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu
Reducibility 08

Slide 16:
REGULARTM is Undecidable
Continue:
If ⟨M, w⟩∈ATM, F(⟨M, w⟩) ∈REGULARTM:
Assume that ⟨M, w⟩∈ATM. Since ⟨M, w⟩∈ATM, M is a TM that accepts w.
Since M accepts w, from the deﬁnition of TM M′, L(M′) = Σ∗. Since
L(M′) = Σ∗, L(M′) is regular. Since L(M′) is regular,
⟨M′⟩∈REGULARTM. Since F(⟨M, w⟩) = ⟨M′⟩,
F(⟨M, w⟩) ∈REGULARTM.
If ⟨M, w⟩̸∈ATM, F(⟨M, w⟩) ̸∈REGULARTM:
Assume that ⟨M, w⟩̸∈ATM. Since ⟨M, w⟩̸∈ATM, M is a TM that does not
accept w. Since M does not accept w, from the deﬁnition of TM M′,
L(M′) = {0n1n| n ≥0}. Since L(M′) = {0n1n| n ≥0}, L(M′) is not
regular. Since L(M′) is not regular, ⟨M′⟩̸∈REGULARTM. Since
F(⟨M, w⟩) = ⟨M′⟩, F(⟨M, w⟩) ̸∈REGULARTM.
This show that TM F is a reduction from ATM to REGULARTM. Therefore
ATM ≤m REGULARTM. Since ATM is undecidable, by Corollary 5.23,
REGULARTM is undecidable.
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu

Time Complexity 01

Slide 2:
Measuring Complexity
A solvable problem takes time to solve
Some problems may take too long to solve
We are going to measure the time to solve a problem by an
algorithm
An algorithm will be represented by a TM
Count the maximum number of steps to process an input of
length n until it halts
Let M be a deterministic Turing machine that halts on all
inputs
The running time or time complexity of M is the function
f : N →N, where f(n) is the maximum number of steps that
M uses on any input of length n
If f(n) is the running time of M, M runs in time f(n)
M is an f(n) time Turing machine
Customarily we use n to represent the length of the input
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu

Slide 3:
Big-O and Small-o Notations
Given a TM M, its running time f(n) may look like the
following:
f(n) = 3n3+ 20n2+ 15n + 12
To eliminate the complexity of a running time of a Turing
machine, we estimate it
This is called asymptotic analysis
Consider only when the Turing machine runs on large input
Consider only the highest order term
Discard the coeﬃcient
We generally use Big-O and Small-o notations
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu
Time Complexity 01

Slide 4:
Big-O Deﬁnition
Let f and g be functions f, g : N →R+
We say that f(n) = O(g(n)) or f(n) is O(g(n)) if positive
integers c and n0 exist such that for every integer n ≥n0,
f(n) ≤cg(n)
When f(n) = O(g(n)):
g(n) is an upper bound for f(n), or
More precisely, g(n) is an asymptotic upper bound for f(n)
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu

Big-O Examples
Show that f(n) = 6n3+ 2n2+ 20n + 45 is O(n3)
Need to ﬁnd c and n0 such that f(n) ≤cn3for n ≥n0
We know the following:
2n2≤2n3for n ≥1
20n ≤20n3for n ≥1
45 ≤45n3for n ≥1
Thus, we have
f(n) = 6n3+ 2n2+ 20n + 45 ≤6n3+ 2n3+ 20n3+ 45n3
= (6 + 2 + 20 + 45)n3
= 73n3for n ≥1
In other words,
f(n) ≤73n3
for n ≥1
From the above equation, what are c and n0?
c = 73
n0 = 1
Thus, 6n3+ 2n2+ 20 + 45 is O(n3)

= 32n log2 n for n ≥1
In other words,
f(n) ≤32n log2 n
for n ≥1

n0 = 1

Slide 11:
Analyzing Algorithms
1 Scan across the tape and reject if a 0 is found to the right of
a 1
This step requires TM M1 to move its tape head all the way
to the right until it encounters a blank symbol and move its
tape head all the way to the left-most square
This step requires either n or 2n steps
In other words, this step is O(n)
2 Repeat if both 0s and 1s remain on the tape:
3
Scan across the tape, crossing oﬀa single 0 and a single 1.
Step 3 requires n steps or O(n)
Cross the ﬁrst 0, move the tape head n/2 step to the ﬁrst 1,
cross it oﬀand move the tape head n/2 step to position on
top of the ﬁrst of the remaining 0s
There will be n/2 0s and n/2 1s
The step 3 is repeated n/2 times
Steps 2 and 3 together is n/2 × O(n) or O(n2).
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu

4 If 0s still remain after all the 1s have been crossed oﬀ, or if 1s
still remain after all the 0s have been crossed oﬀ, reject.
Otherwise, if neither 0s nor 1s remain on the tape, accept.
Simply scan the whole tape looking for a 0 or a 1
This step can be done in n step
This step is O(n)
The running time of M1 is
O(n) + O(n2) + O(n) = O(n2+ 2n) = O(n2)

Time Complexity Class
Deﬁnition 7.7
Let t : N →R+be a function. Deﬁne the time complexity class,
TIME(t(n)), to be the collection of all languages that are
decidable by an O(t(n)) time Turing machine.
From the previous language A = {0k1k| k ≥0} with TM M1
A ∈TIME(n2)
Can we construct a TM M2 such that f(n) = o(n2)?
Asymptotically better than O(n2)
What if we cross oﬀtwo 0s and two 1s in step 3?
Practically faster but not asymptotically better
Step 3 will be repeated n/4 times but n/4 × O(n) = O(n2)

Slide 14:
Asymptotically Faster for Deciding A
Consider TM M2 that decides A = {0k1k| k ≥0}:
M2 = “On input string w:
1
Scan across the tape and reject if a 0 is found to the right of a
1.
2
Repeat as long as some 0s and some 1s remain on the tape.
3
Scan across the tape, checking whether the total number of
0s and 1s remaining is even or odd. If it is odd, reject.
4
Scan again across the tape, crossing oﬀevery other 0
starting with the ﬁrst 0, and then crossing oﬀevery
other 1 starting with the ﬁrst 1.
5
If no 0s and no 1s remain on the tape, accept. Otherwise,
reject.

Slide 15:
Asymptotically Faster for Deciding A
Step 1 and 5 are O(n) as in M1
Step 3, to scan and check whether the number of 0s and 1s
are even or odd is O(n)
In step 4, to cross oﬀevery other 0s and 1s is O(n)
Every time this step is executed, the number of 0s and 1s are
cut in half
If there are n/2 0s and n/2 1s, step 4 needs to be repeated
1 + log2(n/2) times
For example (n = 16), 8 →4 →2 →1 →0 (cut in half 4
times) for input 0818
Steps 2, 3 and 4 all together is
(1 + log2(n/2)) × (O(n) + O(n)) = O(n log n)
Thus, TM M2 run-time is
O(n) + O(n log n) + O(n) = O(n log n)
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu

Slide 16:
Deciding A in Linear Time
The language A can be decided in O(n)
But we need the second tape
Two-tape TM M3 decides A in O(n)
M3 =“On input string w:
1
Scan across tape 1 and reject if a 0 is found to the right of a 1.
2
Scan across the 0s on tape 1 until the ﬁrst 1. At the same
time, copy the 0s onto tape 2.
3
Scan across the 1s on tape 1 until the end of the input. For
each 1 read on tape 1, cross oﬀa 0 on tape 2. If all 0s are
crossed oﬀbefore all the 1s are read, reject.
4
If all the 0s have now been crossed oﬀ, accept. If any 0s
remain, reject.”
Run-time of each step is O(n)
TM M3 runs in O(n)
The run-time complexity of the language A depends on the
model of computation

Complexity Relationships Among Models
Theorem 7.8
Let t(n) be a function where t(n) ≥n. Then every t(n) time
multitape Turing machine has an equivalent O(t2(n)) time
single-tape Turing machine.
Suppose we have a language A that can be decided by a
multitape Turing machine M in t(n) steps where n is the
length of an input string
The above theorem says that there exists a single-tape TM
that can decide A in O(t2(n))
t2(n) = t(n) × t(n)
To prove the above theorem, we simply need to construct a
single tape TM that decides A in O(t2(n))
But what is the language A?
Since we do not know what the language, let’s try to simply
simulate multitape TM using single-tape TM

Slide 19:
Proof of Theorem 7.8
Let M be a k-tape TM for some k ≥2 that runs in t(n) on
input string of length n
We are going to construct a single-tape TM S that simulate
multitape TM M
The ﬁrst step is for S to copy contents of all M’s k tapes into
its tape
Note that contents of all M’s k tapes are ﬁxed except the ﬁrst
one (input tape)
Suppose the length of strings on tape 2 to tape k be a
constant c and the length of input string is n
It requires S to perform c + n + (k + 1) steps to copy
The k + 1 steps are from writing # symbols
Since c and k are constant, this step is O(n)
The next step is to simulate M’s steps
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu
Time Complexity 01

Slide 20:
Proof of Theorem 7.8
For each step of M, ﬁrst S needs to know symbols under M’s
tape heads
S needs to scan and remember all k-dot symbols
Since M performs t(n) steps, the maximum length of an
active portion of each tape is t(n)
If the length of the content of a tape is t(n) + 1, it requires
TM M t(n) step to reach the last symbol
By the time it gets to the last symbol, TM M halts
S needs k × t(n) steps to scan for all k-dot symbols
Scanning for all dot symbols is O(t(n))
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu

Slide 22:
Proof of Theorem 7.8
Note that M performs t(n) steps
Thus, for S to simulate M for t(n) steps, it needs to perform
at most
t(n) × O(t(n)) = O(t2(n))
Therefore, the total run-time is
O(n) + O(t2(n)) = O(t2(n))
where
O(n) steps to copying M’s tape contents, and
O(t2(n)) steps to simulate M’s t(n) steps
If a multitape TM M decides a language A in t(n) steps, we
can simulate TM M using a single tape TM S which can
decide A in O(t2(n))

Time Complexity 01

Slide 24:
Nondeterministic Turing Machine
Theorem 7.11
Let t(n) be a function, where t(n) ≥n. Then every t(n) time
nondeterministic single-tape Turing machine has an equivalent
2O(t(n))time deterministic single-tape Turing machine.
Recall the nondeterministic Turing machine
The computation is a tree
The machine accepts as soon as one of its branch enter the
accept state
The machine rejects if all of its branch are in the reject state
Since we have to consider the maximum number of steps on
any branch, we have to consider the longest number of steps
for the machine to accept or reject an input string of length n
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu

Polynomial Time vs Exponential Time
Polynomial time is considered small but exponential time is
considered large
Consider a problem size n = 1000
Assume that algorithms A and B requires to execute n2and
2ninstructions respectively
Assume that each instruction takes 1 nanosecond
Algorithm A takes
10002× 10−9= 106× 10−9= 10−3= 0.001second
Algorithm B takes
21000× 10−9≈10301× 10−9= 10292seconds ≈10284years

Class P

M = “On input x:
1

2
While this is true

There are two meanings of the word “step” of a TM M:
The above TM has three steps, step 1, step 2, and step 3
Each step takes a number of steps to execute
One step results in a conﬁguration
A TM runs in polynomial time if
1
The number of times each step is executed (including

Slide 5:
Analyzing Algorithms
Recall our Turing machine M1 that decides
A = {0k1k| k ≥0}
M1 = “On input string w:
1
Scan across the tape and reject if a 0 is found to the right of a
1.
2
Repeat if both 0s and 1s remain on the tape:
3
Scan across the tape, crossing oﬀa single 0 and a single 1.
4
If 0s still remain after all the 1s have been crossed oﬀ, or if 1s
still remain after all the 0s have been crossed oﬀ, reject.
Otherwise, if neither 0s nor 1s remain on the tape, accept.”
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu

Slide 6:
Analyzing Algorithm
Input 0k1kwhere k = n/2
Number of times each step is executed:
Step 1 will be executed 1 time
Step 2 and 3 will be executed n/2 times
Step 4 will be executed 1 time
Number of steps of each step
Step 1 takes O(n) steps
Step 2 and 3 take O(n) steps
Step 4 takes O(n) steps
From the analysis:
The number of times each step is executed (including repeat)
is polynomial
Each step takes polynomial time
Therefore, M1 runs in polynomial time
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu

Slide 9:
Brute-Force Algorithm for PATH
The number of possible paths is roughly mm
For each path, you need to check whether it is a path from s
to t
There are roughly mmpaths
To search all possible paths is O(mm)
O(mm) = O((2log2 m)m) = O(2m log2 m)
This will take an exponential time
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu
Time Complexity 02

Slide 10:
Breadth-First Search for PATH
Use breadth-ﬁrst search to achieve polynomial time for PATH:
M = “On input ⟨G, s, t⟩, where G is a directed graph with
nodes s and t:
1
Place a mark on node s.
2
Repeat the following until no additional nodes are marked:
3
Scan all the edges of G. If an edge (a, b) is found going
from a marked node a to an unmarked node b, mark node b.
4
If t is marked, accept. Otherwise, reject.”
For simplicity of analysis, we are going to assume the
following:
The number of nodes is n
The number of edges is proportional to the number of nodes
kn edges for some k
The string representation of a graph is proportional to the
number of nodes and the number of edges
In doing so, we can simply use n (number of nodes) as the
problem size instead of the length of the input string
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu

Slide 11:
Breadth-First Search for PATH
Analysis where input string is ⟨G, s, t⟩where G is a directed
graph and s and t are nodes
Step 1: Place a mark on node s.
This step will be executed one time and the number of steps
is O(n)
Step 4: If t is marked, accept. Otherwise, reject.
This step will be executed one time and the number of steps
is O(n)
Steps 2 and 3:
Runs at most n times (mark one additional node every
repetition)
Recall that there are kn edges
For each execution of step 3, it needs O(kn) = O(n) steps
Conclusion:
Number of times each step will be executed is polynomial
Each step is polynomial
Therefore, this algorithm M is polynomial
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu
Time Complexity 02

Slide 12:
Relatively Prime
Two numbers are relatively prime if 1 is the largest integer
that evenly divides them both
Formally,
RELPRIME = {⟨x, y⟩| x and y are relatively prime}
Is RELPRIME ∈P?
Brute-force algorithm:
Find all possible divisors of both numbers and accept if none
are greater than 1
If a number is represented by n-bit binary, the number of
possible value is 2n
Thus, to ﬁnd all possible divisor of x and y is O(2n)
(exponential)
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu

Slide 13:
Euclidean Algorithm for Greatest Common Divisor
We can check whether x and y are relatively prime by
computing their Greatest Common Divisor (GCD)
If gcd(x, y) = 1, x and y are relatively prime
Computing a gcd can be done using Euclidean Algorithm in a
form of a TM as follows:
E = “On input ⟨x, y⟩, where x and y are natural numbers:
1
Repeat until y = 0:
2
Assign x ←x mod y.
3
Exchange x and y.
4
Output x.”
When we run TM E on input ⟨x, y⟩where x and y are natural
numbers, it simply output their GCD
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu
Time Complexity 02

Slide 14:
Euclidean Algorithm for Greatest Common Divisor
This algorithm R solves RELPRIME
R = “On input ⟨x, y⟩, where x and y are natural numbers in
binary:
1
Run E on ⟨x, y⟩.
2
If the result is 1, accept. Otherwise, reject.
Analysis:
There is no loop in TM R
Step 1 will be executed one time
Step 2 will be executed one time
For the number of steps in each step:
For step 1, it depends on the number of steps of TM E
For step 2, at most O(n)
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu

Slide 15:
RELPRIME ∈P
Analysis of Algorithm E:
Given two numbers x and y, there are two possibilities:
1
x/2 ≥y or
2
x/2 < y
If x/2 ≥y, x mod y < y < x/2
If x/2 < y, x mod y = x −y < x/2
In other words, performing x = x mod y reduces the value of
x roughly in half
Thus, steps 2 and 3 will be repeated roughly 2 log2 x or
2 log2 y (proportional to the length of either x or y)
Multiplied by 2 because of the swap in step 3
Algorithm E runs in polynomial time
RELPRIME ∈P
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu

Hamiltonian Path
A Hamiltonian path in a directed graph G is a directed path
that goes through each node exactly once
Formally,
HAMPATH = {⟨G, s, t⟩| G is a directed graph with a
Hamiltonian path from s to t}

t

Slide 17:
Polynomial Veriﬁability
Unfortunately, we need the brute-force algorithm:
First, we generate all possible paths
Then check each path whether it is a Hamiltonian path from s
to t
This requires exponential time since there are roughly mm
possible paths
No one know an algorithm that can solve Hamiltonian path in
polynomial time yet
No one can prove that the Hamiltonian path problem cannot
be solved in polynomial time yet
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu

Slide 18:
PATH vs HAMPATH
Recall languages PATH and HAMPATH
PATH = {⟨G, s, t⟩| G is a directed graph that has a
directed path from s to t}
HAMPATH = {⟨G, s, t⟩| G is a directed graph with a
Hamiltonian path from s to t}
Given ⟨G, s, t⟩:
to decide whether it is in PATH can be done in polynomial
time
to decide whether it is in HAMPATH (as of now) cannot be
done in polynomial time

Slide 3:
Veriﬁer
Suppose a path is represented by a sequence of pairs of nodes
For example: ((s, a), (a, c), (c, d), (d, r), (r, t))
Given a path c, we can verify whether it is a Hamiltonian path
of graph G from s to t in polynomial time
Given an input ⟨⟨G, s, t⟩, c⟩, where G is a directed graph, s
and t are nodes, and c is a path, a TM can verify whether c is
a Hamiltonian path of graph G from s to t in polynomial time
Similarly, given a natural number c, we can verify whether c is
a factor of a composite number x in polynomial time
Given an input ⟨x, c⟩where x and c are natural numbers, a
TM can verify whether c is a factor of x in polynomial time
This feature is called polynomial veriﬁability
Recall that both problems need exponential time to solve
Verifying is easier than ﬁnding
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu
Time Complexity 03

Slide 4:
Veriﬁer
Recall the language COMPOSITES:
COMPOSITES = {⟨x⟩| x = pq for natural numbers p, q > 1}
We can deﬁne the identical set using a diﬀerent predicate
COMPOSITES = {⟨x⟩| x is divisible by c a natural number c > 1}
Check whether x is divisible by c can be easily done by this
TM V :
V = “On input ⟨x, c⟩where x and c are natural numbers
greater than 1:
1
Calculate y = x mod c
2
If y = 0, accept. If y ̸= 0, reject.”
With the TM V above, now we have
COMPOSITES = {⟨x⟩| V accepts ⟨x, c⟩for some c}
This TM V is called a veriﬁer
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu
Time Complexity 03

Slide 5:
Veriﬁer
A veriﬁer for a language A is an algorithm V , where
A = {w | V accepts ⟨w, c⟩for some string c}
In our discussion, an algorithm V is a TM
If TM V runs in polynomial time, it is called a polynomial
time veriﬁer
A language A is polynomially veriﬁable if it has a polynomial
time veriﬁer
c is called a certiﬁcate or proof of membership in A
Examples:
A certiﬁcate of HAMPATH problem for ⟨G, s, t⟩is simply a
Hamiltonian path from s to t
A certiﬁcate of COMPOSITES problem for a number x is one
of its divisors
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu
Time Complexity 03

Slide 6:
Class NP
Deﬁnition 7.19
NP is the class of languages that have polynomial time veriﬁers.
NP comes from Nondeterministic Polynomial time
NP-Problems are set of problems in NP
Every problems in P also have polynomial time veriﬁer
Therefore, P ⊆NP
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu

000, 001, 010, 011, 100, 101, 110, and 111
This is how we generate all possible strings over Σ = {0, 1} of
length exactly 3

Slide 10:
Nondeterministic TM for HAMPATH
A nondeterministic TM N1 that decides HAMPATH :
N1 = “On input ⟨G, s, t⟩, where G is a directed graph with
nodes s and t:
1
Write a list of m numbers p1, . . . pm, where m is the number
of nodes in G. Each number in the list is nondeterministically
selected to be between 1 and m.
2
Check for repetitions in the list. If any are found reject.
3
Check whether s = p1 and t = pm. If either fail, reject.
4
For each i between 1 and m −1, check whether (pi, pi+1) is
an edge of G. If any are not, reject. Otherwise, all tests have
been passed. so accept.
The nondeterministic part of N1 is in step 1
It generates all permutations of m nodes

Slide 11:
Nondeterministic TM for HAMPATH
What is the run-time of NTM N1?
Step 1 takes only m steps to write m numbers
Check for repetition take O(m2)
Get the ﬁrst number and compare with the rest is O(m)
Get the second number and compare with the rest is O(m)
and so on (m times)
Check whether the ﬁrst number is s and the last number is t is
O(m)
Step 4 takes O(m2)
Get the ﬁrst pair and scan for the edge is O(m)
Get the second pair and scan for the edge is O(m)
and so on (m −1 times)
Note that all copies run steps 2, 3, and 4 simultaneously
The above TM runs in polynomial time
From Theorem 7.11, this algorithm will run in exponential time
on a single-tape Turing machine
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu

equivalent polynomial time nondeterministic Turing machine
(decider)

Slide 13:
Veriﬁer to NTM
Assume that A ∈NP
By deﬁnition, there exists a TM V (a polynomial time veriﬁer)
for the language A that can verify an input of length n in nk
steps for some k
Recall that a veriﬁer for a language A is an algorithm V ,
where
A = {w | V accepts ⟨w, c⟩for some string c}
Construct a NTM N as follows:
N = “On input w of length n:
1
Nondeterministically select string c of length at most nk.
2
Run V on input ⟨w, c⟩.
3
If V accepts, accept; otherwise, reject.”
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu
Time Complexity 03

Slide 14:
NTM to Veriﬁer
Assume that A can be decided by NTM N in polynomial time
Construct a polynomial time veriﬁer V as follows:
V = “On input ⟨w, c⟩, where w and c are strings:
1
Simulate N on input w, treating each symbol of c as a
description of the nondeterministic choice to make at each step
2
If this branch of N’s computation accepts, accept; otherwise,
reject.”
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu

Slide 17:
CLIQUE ∈NP
To show that CLIQUE ∈NP, we can perform one of the
following:
Show a polynomial time veriﬁer V for CLIQUE or
Show an NTM N that decides CLIQUE in polynomial time
The following TM V veriﬁes CLIQUE in polynomial time:
V = “On input ⟨⟨G, k⟩, c⟩:
1
Test whether c is a subgraph with k nodes in G.
2
Test whether G contains all edges connecting nodes in c.
3
If both pass, accept; otherwise, reject.”
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu
Time Complexity 03

Slide 18:
CLIQUE ∈NP
Analysis of TM V :
Each step will be executed only once
Step 1: Need to go back and forth k times
Each time is O(n) step
Thus step 1 is k × O(n) = O(n)
Step 2: for each node a ∈c, it needs to ﬁnd either (a, b) or
(b, a) for every b ∈c and b ̸= a
For each node, it needs to scan back and forth k −1 times
and each time is O(n)
There are k nodes in c. Thus, step 2 is k × (k −1) × O(n) =
O(n)
Thus, V runs in polynomial time
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu

Slide 3:
Polynomial Time Reducibility
Deﬁnition 7.28
A function f : Σ∗→Σ∗is a polynomial time computable
function if some polynomial time Turing machine M exists that
halts with just f(w) on its tape, when started on any input w.
Deﬁnition 7.29
Language A is polynomial time mapping reducible, or simply
polynomial time reducible, to language B, written A ≤p B, if a
polynomial time computable function f : Σ∗→Σ∗exists, where
for every w,
w ∈A ↔f(w) ∈B.
The function f is called the polynomial time reduction of A to
B.
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu
Time Complexity 04

Slide 4:
Polynomial Time Reducibility
Recall the property from previous slide:
w ∈A ↔f(w) ∈B
This is exactly the same as mapping reducibility property
Recall that if A ≤m B and B is decidable, A is decidable
A ≤p B gives you exactly the same as A ≤m B with one
extra property that the reduction f runs in polynomial time
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu

Slide 7:
Polynomial Time Reducibility
This TM MA decides A:
MA = “On input w:
1
Compute f(w) where f is a polynomial time reduction from A
to B.
2
Run MB (a decider of B) on f(w).
3
If MB accepts f(w), accept. If MB rejects f(w), reject.”
Prove that MA is a decider for A:
w ∈A ⇝f(w) ∈B ⇝MB accepts f(w) ⇝MA accepts w
w ̸∈A ⇝f(w) ̸∈B ⇝MB rejects f(w) ⇝MA rejects w
Since f runs in polynomial time and MB can decide B in
polynomial time, MA runs in polynomial time
Since MA decides A in polynomial time, A ∈P
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu

Slide 8:
Satisﬁability Problem
A formula φ can be construed from boolean variables and
boolean operators (∧, ∨, and ¬)
Sometimes we use x instead of ¬x for a boolean variable x
For simplicity, this textbook uses 1 for true and 0 for false
For example, φ = (x ∧y) ∨(x ∧z)
A formula is satisﬁable if there exists an assignment of 0s
and 1s to the variables which makes the formula evaluate to 1
In the above example, φ is 1 when x = 0, y = 1, and z = 0.
The satisﬁability problem is deﬁned formally as follows:
SAT = {⟨φ⟩| φ is a satisﬁable Boolean formula}
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu

Slide 9:
Conjunctive Normal Form (CNF)
We will use a special form of boolean formula called
Conjunctive Normal Form (cnf)
A literal is a Boolean variable or a negation of a boolean
variable, as in x or x
A clause is several literals connected with ∨s
(x1 ∨x2 ∨x3 ∨x4)
A cnf-formula comprises several clauses connected with ∧s
(x1 ∨x2 ∨x3 ∨x4) ∧(x3 ∨x5 ∨x6) ∧(x3 ∨x6)
A 3cnf-formula is a cnf-formula where all the clauses have
exactly three literals, as in
(x1 ∨x2 ∨x3) ∧(x3 ∨x5 ∨x6) ∧(x3 ∨x6 ∨x4) ∧(x4 ∨x5 ∨x6)
Let 3SAT = {⟨φ⟩| φ is a satisﬁable 3cnf-formula}
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu
Time Complexity 04

Slide 10:
3SAT ≤p CLIQUE
Is 3SAT ≤p CLIQUE?
We need to create a polynomial time reduction
f : Σ∗→Σ∗such that
w ∈3SAT ↔f(w) ∈CLIQUE
Recall 3SAT and CLIQUE:
3SAT = {⟨φ⟩| φ is a satisﬁable 3cnf-formula}
CLIQUE = {⟨G, k⟩| G is an undirected graph with a k-clique}
These are two totally diﬀerent problems
We need to convert ⟨φ⟩to ⟨G, k⟩such that φ is satisﬁable if
and only if graph G contains k-clique for some k
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu

Slide 11:
3SAT ≤p CLIQUE
A 3cnf-formula is of the form φ = c1 ∧c2 ∧. . . cn for some
n ≥1
φ is evaluated to 1 if ci = 1 for every 1 ≤i ≤n
Every clause ci in φ depends on other clauses cj in φ to be 1
to make φ evaluated to 1
Each clause c in a 3cnf-formula is of the form
c = (xp ∨xq ∨xr)
for literals xp, xq, and xr
The clause c is evaluated to 1 if one of its literal is 1
If a literal in a clause is 1, for φ to be evaluated to 1, it
depends on at least one literal in other clauses to be 1
Note that it cannot depend on its negation literals
It does not depend on the other two literals in its clause since
its clause is already evaluated to 1
This create relations among n literals (one from each clause)
which can be represented using edges in an indirected graph
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu
Time Complexity 04

Slide 12:
3SAT ≤p CLIQUE
Let φ be a formula with k clauses as
φ = (a1 ∨b1 ∨c1) ∧(a2 ∨b2 ∨c2) ∧· · · ∧(ak ∨bk ∨ck)
Nodes in G are organized in k groups of tree nodes each call
triples
Each triple corresponds to one of the clauses in φ
Each node in a triple corresponds to a literal in its associated
clause
Edges connect every two nodes in G except the following:
No edge among nodes in the same triple
No edge is present between two nodes with contradictory
labels, as in x2 and x2
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu

NP-Completeness
Recall that NP-Complete is a class of problems that are
closely related
If you can solve one problem in this class in polynomial time,
all problems in this class can be solved in polynomial time
Deﬁnition 7.34
A language B is NP-Complete if it satisﬁes two conditions:
1 B is in NP, and
2 Every language A in NP is polynomial time reducible to B.

reducible to B

About NTM M that decides A
If M accepts w ⇝w ∈A ⇝the accepting computation
history for M on input w exists
If M rejects w ⇝w ̸∈A ⇝the accepting computation history
for M on input w does not exists
This is an NTM, there can be multiple computation histories
We need to use reduction via computation histories
Convert computation histories of M on input w to a formula φ
such that
If the accepting computation history for M on input w exists,
φ is satisﬁable (φ ∈SAT)
If the accepting computation history for M on input w does
not exists, φ is not satisﬁable (φ ̸∈SAT)
Cook-Levin Theorem shows the method describe above and
conclude that SAT is NP-Complete.
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu
Time Complexity 04

Slide 20:
3SAT is NP-Complete
Since we already know that SAT is NP-Complete, to show
that 3SAT is NP-Complete, we need to show the following:
1
3SAT ∈NP:
Construct an NTM that generates all possible assignments
If one of them evaluate to 1, accept
2
SAT ≤p 3SAT:
Convert the formula φ into a 3cnf f(φ) satisfying
φ ∈SAT ↔f(φ) ∈3SAT
where f is a polynomial time reduction from SAT to 3SAT
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu
Time Complexity 04

Slide 21:
From SAT to 3SAT
If a clause has less than three literal, add one or two literals
from the same clause
x1 ⇝(x1 ∨x1 ∨x1)
(x1 ∨x2) ⇝(x1 ∨x1 ∨x2)
(x1 ∨x2) ⇝(x1 ∨x2 ∨x2)
These results are logically equivalent with their original clauses
If a clause has more than tree literals, split it into a number of
clauses of three literals each.
(x1 ∨x2 ∨x3 ∨x4) ⇝(x1 ∨x2 ∨z) ∧(z ∨x3 ∨x4)
This is not logically equivalent
It is equivalent in terms of satisﬁability
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu
Time Complexity 04

Slide 22:
Additional NP-Complete Problems
CLIQUE is NP-Complete
We already show that 3SAT ≤p CLIQUE
VERTEX −COVER is NP-Complete where
VERTEX −COVER = {⟨G, k⟩| G is an undirected graph that
has a k-node vertex cover}
Textbook shows that 3SAT ≤p VERTEX −COVER
HAMPATH is NP-Complete
Textbook shows that 3SAT ≤p HAMPATH
SUBSET−SUM is NP-Complete
Textbook shows that 3SAT ≤p SUBSET−SUM
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu
Time Complexity 04

Slide 2:
PSUBSETDFA
Question
Prove that
PSUBSETDFA = {⟨A, B⟩|A and B are DFAs and L(A) ⊂L(B)}
is decidable.
Recall that you need to perform two tasks:
1
Construct a TM that you think it decides PSUBSETDFA
2
Prove that your TM is actually a decider for PSUBSETDFA
Recall L(A) ⊂L(B) (proper subset)
L(A) ̸= L(B) and L(A) is a subset of L(B)
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu

input:
Slide 3:
PSUBSETDFA is Decidable
Solution: Construct a TM M′to decide PSUBSETDFA as follows:
M′=“On input ⟨A, B⟩where A and B are DFAs:
1
Run TM F on input ⟨A, B⟩
2
If F accepts ⟨A, B⟩, reject.
3
Construct a DFA C such that L(C) = L(A) ∩L(B)
4
Run TM T on input ⟨C⟩
5
If T accepts ⟨C⟩, accept. If T rejects ⟨C⟩, reject.”
Next, we need to prove that the above TM M′is a decider for PSUBSETDFA.
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu
Review

Slide 4:
PSUBSETTM is Decidable
To show that TM M′is a decider for PSUBSETDFA, we need to perform the
following:
Show that if ⟨A, B⟩∈PSUBSETDFA, M′will accept ⟨A, B⟩.
Assume that ⟨A, B⟩∈PSUBSETDFA. Since ⟨A, B⟩∈PSUBSETDFA, A and B
are DFAs and L(A) is a proper subset of L(B). In other words, L(A) is not
equal to L(B) and L(A) is a subset of L(B). Since L(A) is not equal to L(B),
⟨A, B⟩̸∈EQDFA. Since F is a decider for EQDFA, by running F on input ⟨A, B⟩,
F will reject ⟨A, B⟩. Since L(A) is a subset of L(B), L(C) will be empty. Since
L(C) is empty, ⟨C⟩∈EDFA. Since T is a decider for EDFA, by running T on
input ⟨C⟩, T will accept ⟨C⟩. Since T accepts ⟨C⟩, M′accepts ⟨A, B⟩.
Show that if ⟨A, B⟩̸∈PSUBSETTM, M′will reject ⟨A, B⟩.
Assume that ⟨A, B⟩̸∈PSUBSETDFA. Since ⟨A, B⟩̸∈PSUBSETDFA, A and B
are DFAs and L(A) is not a proper subset of L(B). In other words,
L(A) = L(B) or L(A) is not a subset of L(B).
Suppose L(A) = L(B). Since L(A) = L(B), ⟨A, B⟩∈EQDFA. Since F is a
decider for EQDFA, by running F on input ⟨A, B⟩, F will accept ⟨A, B⟩. Since
F accepts ⟨A, B⟩, M′rejects ⟨A, B⟩.
Suppose L(A) is not a subset of L(B). Since L(A) is not a subset of L(B),
L(C) will not be empty. Since L(C) is not empty, ⟨C⟩̸∈EDFA. Since T is a
decider for EDFA, by running T on input ⟨C⟩, T will reject ⟨C⟩. Since T rejects
⟨C⟩, M′rejects ⟨A, B⟩.
This shows that M′is a decider for PSUBSETDFA DFA. Therefore, PSUBSETDFA is
decidable.
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu
Review

input:
Slide 5:
ALLBUTONETM
Question
Prove that
ALLBUTONETM = {⟨M⟩|M is a TM that accepts all but one string}
is undecidable. Note that if TM M accepts all but one string,
L(M) = Σ∗−{w} for a string w.
Recall that there are two methods:
1
Show that if ALLBUTONETM is decidable, ATM is decidable
2
Use mapping reducibility to show that
ATM ≤m ALLBUTONETM
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu
Review

Slide 6:
ALLBUTONETM is Undecidable (Method 1)
Assume that ALLBUTONETM is decidable. Since ALLBUTONETM is decidable, there
exists a TM R that decides ALLBUTONETM. Construct a TM S to decide ATM as
follows:
S =“On input ⟨M, w⟩where M is a TM and w is a string:
1
Construct a TM M′as follows:
M′=“On input x:
1
If x == ε, reject.
2
Run M on input w.
3
If M accepts w, accept. If M rejects w, reject.”
2
Run TM R on input ⟨M′⟩.
3
If R accepts ⟨M′⟩, accept. If R rejects ⟨M′⟩, reject.”
Next, we need to show that the above TM S is a decider for ATM.
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu
Review

input:
Slide 7:
ALLBUTONETM is Undecidable (Method 1)
To show that TM M′is a decider for ALLBUTONETM, we need to perform the
following:
If ⟨M, w⟩∈ATM, S will accept ⟨M, w⟩.
Assume that ⟨M, w⟩∈ATM. Since ⟨M, w⟩∈ATM, M is a TM that accepts w.
Since M accepts w, from the description of TM M′, L(M′) = Σ∗−{ε}. Since
L(M′) = Σ∗−{ε}, ⟨M′⟩∈ALLBUTONETM. Since R is a decider for
ALLBUTONETM, by running R on input ALLBUTONETM, R will accept ⟨M′⟩.
Since R accepts ⟨M′⟩, S accepts ⟨M, w⟩.
If ⟨M, w⟩̸∈ATM, S will reject ⟨M, w⟩.
Assume that ⟨M, w⟩̸∈ATM. Since ⟨M, w⟩̸∈ATM, M is a TM that does not
accept w. Since M does not accept w, from the description of TM M′,
L(M′) = ∅. Since L(M′) = ∅, L(M′) ̸= Σ∗−{w} for a string w. In other
words, ⟨M′⟩̸∈ALLBUTONETM. Since R is a decider for ALLBUTONETM, by
running R on input ALLBUTONETM, R will reject ⟨M′⟩. Since R rejects ⟨M′⟩,
S rejects ⟨M, w⟩.
This shows that S is a decider for ATM. In other words ATM is decidable. This
contradicts with the fact that ATM is undecidable. Therefore, ALLBUTONETM is
undecidable.
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu
Review

Slide 8:
ALLBUTONETM is Undecidable (Method 2)
Need to show that ATM ≤m ALLBUTONETM. Construct a TM F as follows:
F =“On input ⟨M, w⟩where M is a TM and w is a string:
1
Construct a TM M′as follows:
M′=“On input x:
1
If x == ε, reject.
2
Run M on input w.
3
If M accepts w, accept. If M rejects w, reject.”
2
Output ⟨M′⟩.
Next, we need to show that the above TM F is a reduction from ATM to
ALLBUTONETM. In other word, we have to show that it satisﬁes:
⟨M, w⟩∈ATM ↔F(⟨M, w⟩) ∈ALLBUTONETM
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu
Review

input:
Slide 9:
ALLBUTONETM is Undecidable (Method 2)
To show that TM F is a reduction from ATM to ALLBUTONETM, we need to perform
the following:
If ⟨M, w⟩∈ATM, F(⟨M, w⟩) ∈ALLBUTONETM.
Assume that ⟨M, w⟩∈ATM. Since ⟨M, w⟩∈ATM, M is a TM that accepts w.
Since M accepts w, from the description of TM M′, L(M′) = Σ∗−{ε}. Since
L(M′) = Σ∗−{ε}, ⟨M′⟩∈ALLBUTONETM. Since F(⟨M, w⟩) = ⟨M′⟩,
F(⟨M, w⟩) ∈ALLBUTONETM.
If ⟨M, w⟩̸∈ATM, F(⟨M, w⟩) ̸∈ALLBUTONETM.
Assume that ⟨M, w⟩̸∈ATM. Since ⟨M, w⟩̸∈ATM, M is a TM that does not
accept w. Since M does not accept w, from the description of TM M′,
L(M′) = ∅. Since L(M′) = ∅, L(M′) ̸= Σ∗−{w} for a string w. In other
words, ⟨M′⟩̸∈ALLBUTONETM. Since F(⟨M, w⟩) = ⟨M′⟩,
F(⟨M, w⟩) ̸∈ALLBUTONETM.
This shows that F is a reduction from ATM to ALLBUTONETM. Since ATM is
undecidable, ALLBUTONETM is undecidable.
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu
Review

Slide 10:
NOTPRIME
Question
Consider the following language
NOTPRIME = {⟨n⟩|n is a natural number and n is not a prime number}
Perform the following:
1 Construct a veriﬁer for the language NOTPRIME
2 Construct an NTM that decides NOTPRIME
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu

input:
Slide 11:
A Veriﬁer of NOTPRIME
Given a language A, a veriﬁer V of the language A is a TM
satisfying
A = {w | V accepts ⟨w, c⟩for some c}
From the above description
Given a string w ∈A, V must accept ⟨w, c⟩for some c
Given a string w ̸∈A, V must reject ⟨w, c⟩for all c
A veriﬁer for NOTPRIME must satisﬁes
NOTPRIME = {⟨n⟩| V accepts ⟨n, c⟩for some c}
An example of a veriﬁer for the language NOTPRIME:
V =“On input ⟨n, c⟩where n and c are natural numbers:
1
If c == 1 or c ≥n, reject.
2
If n mod c == 0, accept.”
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu
Review

Slide 12:
An NTM that decides NOTPRIME
Think about how brute force algorithm should work
Generate all possible strings between 2 and n −1 (inclusive)
If n is divisible by one of them, n is not a prime number
An example of an NTM that decides the language
NOTPRIME:
N =“On input ⟨n⟩where n is a natural numbers:
1
Select a number c where c is nondeterministically selected
between 2 and n −1 (inclusive)
2
If n mod c == 0, accept.”
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu
Review

Slide 5:
What does an OS do?
• Manages (controls and arbitrates) resources
•
Processors, Memory, Input/output devices,
Communication devices, Storage, Software applications
•
Conflicting goals:
•
e.g., performance vs. utilization
•
Separation of policy and mechansim
• Provides abstractions to application programs
•
Ease of use
•
Virtualization
• Protects resources
CS 1550 – Operating Systems – Sherif Khattab

Slide 6:
Interrupts
Hardware or software:
•
Hardware interrupt by one of the devices
•
Software interrupt (exception or trap):
• Software error (e.g., division by zero)
• Other process problems include processes trying to modify each
other’s or the operating system’s memory (e.g., segmentation
fault)
• Request for operating system service (i.e., system call)
CS 1550 – Operating Systems – Sherif Khattab

input:
Slide 7:
Interrupt Descriptor Table
• Interrupt transfers control to the interrupt service
routine (ISR)
• ISRs are segments of code that determine what
action should be taken for each type of interrupt
•
part of the OS kernel
• An interrupt vector contains the address of the ISR
for one interrupt
• An interrupt vector table is an array of interrupt
vectors
•
also known as interrupt descriptor table (IDT)
CS 1550 – Operating Systems – Sherif Khattab

Slide 8:
Dual-mode Operation
• Dual-mode operation allows OS to protect itself and
other system components
•
At least two modes: user mode and kernel mode
•
Mode bit(s) provided by hardware (inside CPU registers)
• Provides ability to distinguish when system is running user code
or kernel code
• Some instructions designated as privileged, only executable in
kernel mode
• Some memory addresses designated as privileged, only
accessible in kernel mode
•
Therefore, we get segmentation fault on null (i.e., 0) pointer dereference
• Interrupts change mode to kernel
•
return from interrupt resets mode back to user
• Increasingly CPUs support multi-mode operations
•
virtual machine manager (VMM) mode for guest VMs
CS 1550 – Operating Systems – Sherif Khattab

Slide 9:

Slide 13:
System Calls
• Programming interface to OS services
• Typically written in a high-level language (C or C++)
• Mostly accessed by programs via a high-level
Application Programming Interface (API) rather
than direct system call use
•
Win32 API for Windows
•
POSIX API for POSIX-based systems (including virtually
all versions of UNIX, Linux, and Mac OS X), and
•
Java API for the Java virtual machine (JVM)
CS 1550 – Operating Systems – Sherif Khattab

Slide 14:
System Call Implementation
•
Typically, there is a number associated with each system
call
•
Each system call has a corresponding system call
implementation function (part of the OS kernel)
•
System-call table indexed according to these numbers
•
Each entry in the table contains the address of the
corresponding system call implementation function
•
The system call interface is the ISR corresponding to the
syscall software interrupt
•
invokes the intended system call in OS kernel,
•
passes arguments if needed, and
•
returns status of the system call and any return values
•
The caller need know nothing about how the system call
is implemented
CS 1550 – Operating Systems – Sherif Khattab

Slide 18:
System Call Parameter Passing
• Three general methods used to pass parameters to
the OS
•
Simplest:  pass the parameters in registers
•
In some cases, may be more parameters than registers
•
Parameters stored in a block, or table, in memory, and
address of block passed as a parameter in a register
• This approach taken by Linux and Solaris
•
Parameters placed, or pushed, onto the stack by the
program and popped off the stack by the operating
system
•
XV6
• Block and stack methods do not limit the number or
length of parameters being passed

Slide 9:
Process Control Block (PCB)
Information associated with each process
(also called task control block)
•
Process state – running, waiting, etc
•
Program counter – location of instruction to execute next
•
CPU registers – contents of all process-centric registers
•
CPU scheduling information- priorities, scheduling queue
pointers
•
Memory-management information – memory allocated to
the process
•
Accounting information – CPU used, clock time elapsed
since start, time limits
•
I/O status information – I/O devices allocated to process,
list of open files
CS/COE 1550 – Operating Systems – Sherif Khattab
9

Slide 10:
Process Representation in Linux
Represented by the C structure task_struct
pid_t pid; /* process identifier */
long state; /* state of the process */
unsigned int time_slice /* scheduling information */
struct task_struct *parent; /* this process’s parent */
struct list_head children; /* this process’s children */
struct files_struct *files; /* list of open files */
struct mm_struct *mm; /* address space of this process */
CS/COE 1550 – Operating Systems – Sherif Khattab

Slide 11:
Context Switch
• When CPU switches to another process, the system
must save the state of the old process and load the
saved state for the new process via a context
switch
• Context of a process represented in the PCB
• Context-switch time is overhead; the system does no
useful work while switching
•
The more complex the OS and the PCB the longer the
context switch
• Time dependent on hardware support
•
Some hardware provides multiple sets of registers per
CPU multiple contexts loaded at once
CS/COE 1550 – Operating Systems – Sherif Khattab

input:
Slide 5:
Today’s problem: Busy Waiting
• A process that is trying to acquire a locked spinlock is
running!
•
It continuously checks:
• can I get the lock? No, lock is held by another process
• can I get the lock? No, lock is held by another process
• …
•
This continuous check is called spinning or busy waiting
•
But what is wrong with that?
• Busy waiting wastes CPU cycles
• on a single-core system it delays the process that is holding the
lock from releasing it
CS/COE 1550 – Operating Systems – Sherif Khattab
5

Slide 6:
Today’s problem: Busy Waiting
While P1 is in the critical region, P2 is busy waiting
CS/COE 1550 – Operating Systems – Sherif Khattab
6
Process P1
lock(lk);
//critical region (e.g., x++)
unlock(lk);
Shared Data
Spinlock lk;
int x;
Process P2
lock(lk);
//critical region (e.g., x++)
unlock(lk);

Slide 15:
Semaphores
•
Solution: use semaphores
•
Synchronization mechanism that doesn’t require busy waiting
•
Implementation
•
Semaphore S accessed by two atomic operations
•
Down(S): decrement the semaphore if > 0; block otherwise
•
Up(S): increment the semaphore and wakeup one blocked process if any
•
Down() is another name for P()
•
Up() is another name for V()
CS/COE 1550 – Operating Systems – Sherif Khattab

Types of semaphores
• Two different types of semaphores
•
Counting semaphores
•
Binary semaphores
• Counting semaphore
•
Value can range over an unrestricted range
• Binary semaphore
•
Only two values possible
• 1 means the semaphore is available
• 0 means a process has acquired the semaphore
•
May be simpler to implement
• Possible to implement one type using the other

Slide 8:
Semaphore Usage Problem: Deadlock and Starvation
•
Deadlock: two or more processes are
waiting indefinitely for an event that can
only be caused by a waiting process
•
P0 gets A, needs B
•
P1 gets B, needs A
•
Each process waiting for the other to signal
•
Starvation: indefinite blocking
•
Process is never removed from the
semaphore queue in which its suspended
•
May be caused by ordering in queues
(priority)

Optimization trick:
• copy-on-write

Slide 15:
Process Termination
• Via exit(), abort(), or kill() syscalls
• The parent process may wait for termination of a
child process by using the wait()system call.The
call returns status information and the pid of the
terminated process
pid = wait(&status);
• When a process terminates
•
If no parent waiting (did not invoke wait()) process is a
zombie
•
If parent terminated without invoking wait , process is an
orphan
• adopted by the init process

Slide 27:
Monitors
•
A monitor is another kind of high-level synchronization primitive
•
One monitor has multiple entry points
•
Think multiple methods in a Java class
•
Only one process may be in the monitor at any time
•
Enforces mutual exclusion - less chance for programming errors
•
Monitors provided by high-level language
•
Variables belonging to monitor are protected from simultaneous access
•
Procedures in monitor are guaranteed to have mutual exclusion
CS 1550 – Operating Systems – Sherif Khattab
27

Slide 28:
Monitor usage
•
Supported by Java
•
synchronized keyword
•
Only one process can be executing in either produce or consume at any time
CS 1550 – Operating Systems – Sherif Khattab

input:
Slide 29:
Condition variables in monitors
• Problem: how can a process wait inside a monitor?
•
Can’t simply sleep: there’s no way for anyone else to
enter
•
Solution: use a condition variable
• Condition variables support two operations
•
Wait(): suspend this process until signaled
•
Signal(): wake up exactly one process waiting on this
condition variable
• If no process is waiting, signal has no effect
• Signals on condition variables aren’t “saved up”
•
Different from semaphores!
• Condition variables are only usable within monitors
•
Process must be in monitor to signal on a condition
variable
CS 1550 – Operating Systems – Sherif Khattab
29

Slide 30:
Locks & condition variables
•
Monitors require native language support
•
What if native support is not there (e.g., in C)?
•
Provide monitor support using special data types and procedures
•
Locks (Acquire(), Release())
•
Condition variables (Wait(), Signal())
•
Lock usage
•
Acquiring a lock == entering a monitor
•
Releasing a lock == leaving a monitor
•
Condition variable usage
•
Each condition variable is associated with exactly one lock
•
Lock must be held to use condition variable
•
Waiting on a condition variable releases the lock implicitly and reacquires the
lock upon returning
CS 1550 – Operating Systems – Sherif Khattab
